item.title: Tech Bulls Ready to Buy as Rout Crushes Valuations
item.description: Nasdaq 100 valuation relative to S&P 500 is lowest since 2018.

item.title: Zhipu’s Losses Climb 60% After Chinese AI Rivalry Worsens
item.description: Zhipu reported a much faster-than-expected 60% surge in net losses for 2025, underscoring how China’s AI upstarts continue to spend aggressively on developing artificial intelligence to keep pace with rivals.

item.title: ECB’s Vujcic Says Rise in Inflation Expectations Isn’t Surprise
item.description: European Central Bank Governing Council member Boris Vujcic said the increase in inflation expectations since the Iran war broke out isn’t a surprise.

item.title: Pakistan Weighs Hormuz Options After Iran Clears 20 Ships
item.description: Pakistan is considering options that include allowing other ships to ferry vital cargoes under its flag, after Iran said it would allow 20 of the nation’s vessels through the Strait of Hormuz — more than it currently has in the area.

item.title: Whoop Raises $575 Million at a $10 Billion Valuation on Its Way to an IPO
item.description: Whoop Inc., the maker of popular screenless fitness bands, is now valued at $10.1 billion after raising an additional $575 million, a new milestone on its way to an initial public offering.

item.title: Ramaphosa Courts Investment as South Africa Faces Iran War Headwinds
item.description: President Cyril Ramaphosa pitched South Africa as a reform-driven investment haven at a time of increasing global uncertainty, as he set a target off attracting 2 trillion rand ($117 billion) of capital to the country over the next five years.

item.title: Credit Data Firm 9fin Is Valued at $1.3 Billion in Funding Round
item.description: 9fin Ltd., the debt intelligence provider founded by two former investment banking analysts, has raised fresh funds at a $1.3 billion valuation as part of a push to capture a bigger share of the hotly contested market for credit research.

item.title: ECB’s Muller Can’t Rule Out Interest-Rate Hike in April
item.description: European Central Bank Governing Council member Madis Muller said he can’t exclude an increase in borrowing costs at next month’s policy meeting should the Iran war keep oil and natural gas prices elevated.

item.title: JD.com-Backed Group Is Said to Pick Banks for IPO of Singapore REIT
item.description: A unit of e-commerce company JD.com Inc. and partners have picked financial advisers including Bank of America Corp., DBS Group Holdings Ltd. and UBS Group AG for an initial public offering of their Singapore-based real estate investment trust, according to people with knowledge of the matter.

item.title: South Africa to Lower Fuel Levy as Gasoline Price Spike Looms
item.description: South Africa will reduce a tax imposed on fuel to offset the impact of surging oil prices on domestic gasoline, Finance Minister Enoch Godongwana said.

item.title: Turkey Restarts FX Swaps to Counter War Impact on Reserves
item.description: Turkey restarted foreign-currency swap transactions with local banks for the first time in a year, after a drawdown in reserves fueled by a broad selloff across emerging markets amid the war in the Middle East.

item.title: Euro-Zone Inflation Jumps Most Since 2022 as War Drives Energy
item.description: The euro area saw its steepest jump in inflation since 2022 as the Iran war pushed energy costs sharply higher, backing expectations that the European Central Bank will have to raise interest rates.

item.title: Airbnb Adds Third-Party Private Car Pickups in Latest Services Push
item.description: Airbnb Inc. is offering private car pickups in major markets outside the US, the latest move in an ambitious plan to offer hotel-like services in addition to lodgings.

item.title: Peru’s Crowded Election Leaves Room for a Surprise Contender
item.description: With 35 candidates and about a third of Peruvians undecided or backing no one, a surprise contender could yet emerge in the final days ahead of the April 12 vote.

item.title: President Gustavo Petro’s ‘Worst Mistake’ Saves Colombia Central Bank From Turkey’s Fate
item.description: Colombian President Gustavo Petro says that naming Olga Lucía Acosta to the central bank was his biggest blunder, after she resisted his calls to slash interest rates. For the same reason, others credit her with saving the bank’s independence and the nation’s inflation-targeting model.

item.title: Oil Shock Tests Bank of Thailand’s Wait-and-See Policy Stance
item.description: Thailand’s central bank signaled a wait-and-see monetary policy approach, saying interest-rate cuts are unlikely to be effective against a Middle East-driven oil shock while leaving the door open to tightening if inflation pressures persist.

item.title: China Seeks Better EU Ties as Lawmakers Return After Eight Years
item.description: China welcomed the first visit by European lawmakers in eight years, as Beijing seeks to stabilize relations following the lifting of retaliatory sanctions last year.

item.title: European Rates Set Volatility Record as Algos Amplify War Swings
item.description: European rates markets are set for the most volatile month on record, sparking chatter over the role robot traders are playing in exacerbating wild swings during the Middle East conflict.

item.title: China’s Midea Is Said to Mull $2 Billion Convertible Bond Sale
item.description: Chinese appliance maker Midea Group Co. is considering a potential convertible bond offering that could raise as much as about $2 billion, people familiar with the matter said, as sales of such notes surge.

item.title: Iran Conflict Clouds South African Economic Outlook, SARB Says
item.description: The Iran war has clouded the outlook for a South African economy that had entered its longest period of economic expansion since 2018, the country’s central bank said.

item.title: Syngenta Boosts Profit Before Potential IPO in Hong Kong
item.description: Syngenta Group, the Chinese-owned seed and pesticide giant that’s planning a potential Hong Kong listing, increased profits as it focused on higher-margin businesses.

item.title: ECB’s Muller Says Probable Rates Will Rise in Coming Quarters
item.description: European Central Bank Governing Council Madis Muller said it’s likely that borrowing costs will have to be increased over the coming quarters due to the inflationary effect of the Iran war.

item.title: Why NASA Hired a Chief Economist
item.description: Evaluating the economic impact of space travel.

item.title: Odd Lots: Why NASA Hired a Chief Economist (Podcast)
item.description: Odd Lots: Why NASA Hired a Chief Economist (Podcast)【原文：Odd Lots: Why NASA Hired a Chief Economist (Podcast)】

item.title: Brookfield Buys €1 Billion of Spanish Housing from Blackstone
item.description: Brookfield Asset Management has finalized a deal to buy a portfolio of rental housing from Blackstone Inc. for over €1 billion ($1.1 billion), the largest real estate transaction in Europe since the US and Israel launched their attack on Iran.

item.title: SNB Sold Currencies in Final Quarter in Potential Buoy for Franc
item.description: The Swiss National Bank sold a small amount of foreign exchange at the end of last year, slightly reducing its balance sheet while risking a strengthening in the franc.

item.title: Poland’s Inflation Rebounds as Iran War Boosts Fuel Prices
item.description: Polish inflation rebounded after energy prices soared from the Iran war, in what may prompt caution from central bankers after their bold interest rate cut just days after the start of hostilities.

item.title: Tokyo Bourse Gives Last Warning to 25 Firms at Risk of Delisting
item.description: The Tokyo Stock Exchange placed 25 companies under supervision in a final step toward a wave of delistings.

item.title: Malaysia Says Its Tankers Will Be Exempt from Iran’s Hormuz Toll
item.description: Malaysian tankers allowed to sail through the Strait of Hormuz won’t be subject to a toll imposed by Iran, according to Transport Minister Anthony Loke.

item.title: Japanese Investors Back New VC Fund Targeting African Startups
item.description: Japanese companies including Mitsubishi Corp., Sumitomo Mitsui Banking Corp. and Toyota Ventures LLC invested in a $147 million Africa-focused venture capital fund, in a sign of growing interest from the Asian nation in the continent’s startups.
